sentences.text: Right.
And this was already the 60 year since 1939 to 1944, five years of war that was supposed to be a blitz war.
And I mentioned already that I came to Czestochowa, where they transported the ammunition factories.
And we were still there working on-- and in Czestochowa, I was also together with a group.
We found each other.
And had this spiritual resistance between-- between us.
In fact, there was [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was also a brother of Bashevis Singer in our group.
I was the youngest one.
Pardon?
Bashevis Singer is the Noble Prize writer that recently passed away in America, who he wrote many books from shtetls, and so on.
The time seemed to be nearing, and we were hoping for the liberation.
But it would be too good to happen.
So in the middle of January, they--
1945 they liquidated the camp in Czestochowa and took part of the-- first, they deported the men.
And then they took the-- the women in a transport to Germany.
We passed Buchenwald, where they let off the men.
And while I was in the cattle train looking out the window, I talked with a prisoner.
I say, can you save any of your things?
Do they leave anything?
So he said, no.
They take everything.
Pictures too?
They told me, yes.
And when our trip continued in the cattle cars, I took the pictures that I had.
And I cut out the head of my family, my brother, even a picture of myself, because I wanted to identify myself by myself.
Because since I smuggled out from my home town, I was never with people that know me-- who I was from home, and so on.
And I put all those little heads that I cut.
And I had a family picture-- a square inch-- the size-- that I cut out.
And I put it in.
And the group picture from my [NON-ENGLISH] I rolled in.
I put in all in the tubes.
The-- the picture of my group there too.
I cut around the white-- just left the heads of my friends there.
And when I came through Bergen-Belsen, and they told us to disrobe, I put my picture tube in my rectum.
This was a celluloid tube that I had the valuable things that my mother gave me when I left, with the poison pill from my brother, as I mentioned before.
I took out the valuable stones.
I left the poison pill.
And I put in the pictures that I cut out in the cattle train in the tube.
And when I arrived in Bergen-Belsen, and we had to disrobe ourselves, they even told us to keep the clothes right and keep it good.
And we are going to come back and put it on.
But I knew that this wasn't the case, because I was told about it in Buchenwald.
And I put the tube in my rectum when I had to pass the shower-- the disinfection shower.
And actually, when we came out from the shower, we got striped coats.
And I remember under the coat, they gave me a purple evening dress, and no underwear, and wooden shoes.
And this was the height of winter.
And I remember when we went out, my hair were wet.
And they stood up like ice.
And we stood there on long appells, which is counting sessions.
Many people got sick.
Many people died.
In Bergen-Belsen, our time was spent just carrying stones back and forth just to wear it out-- wear us out.
They didn't even want to waste a-- a bullet on us.
Later on, I was four weeks in Bergen-Belsen, and I went out with the last transport from Bergen-Belsen.
There were 500 women and 500 men.
We were moved to Southern Germany to a work place.
In the middle of our route, the train tracks were bumped.
And for 14 days, we stood on Wurzburg, in Wurzburg, Main-- this was a city in Germany.
And we couldn't move.
And a lot of people died from starvation.
Wurzburg-- I think it is W-U- umlaut-- R-T-Z-B-U-G.
--dash Main.
I think it is.
I don't know how you spell it-- M-A-I-N or M-E-I-N.
The Main is the river-- a river that we were there.
And we stood there.
And we got rations for four days-- a bread, and conserves, and so on.
But we were there 14 days.
A lot of people ate it up in one day, and they got sick from the food.
They said, I'm going to be three days hungry, but one day I want to be full.
And a lot of people died from the food there.
And the other people, if they ate it so fast, they had so many more days to starve.
I kept the food a little longer, and I didn't have so many starvation days.
Anyhow, I came.
And there was less than 1/2 of the people that survived that transport.
This was just my willpower, I imagine, and not to give-- give in to that overpowering hunger.
I even remember giving away a piece of bread to one of the girls.
She was so thin that I could see all her veins through the skin.
And she was one of the girls from Krakow that I knew her relatives.
She was a relative of [NON-ENGLISH].
And I gave her.
I didn't help her much, but it was just my feeling.
Then we arrived with the transport to Burgau.
And this was a airplane factory.
Burgau-- I don't know.
It is B-U-- B-U-R-G-A-U.
And this was in Southern Germany, in Bayern.
And this was a camp that was-- belonged to one of the camps that belonged to Dachau.
And I was in assembly line.
I was light and small, so I was on the wings of the airplane putting in rivets, and was there in the assembly line.
And we were working there with some of the French war prisoners.
And they sometimes helped us with a piece of bread.
And they filled us in-- in the war news.
Later from that camp, in the beginning of April, we were transport to Turkheim.
This was a camp that was just the concentration camp.
We didn't do any work there.
We just--
Turkheim was T-U- umlaut-- K-H-E-I-M.
And this belonged to Dachau too.
In that camp, the end of April, they were trying to transport everybody to Dachau with intention to kill everybody-- liquidate.
The Germans were hoping that there is not going to be a witness left.
And when we walked out of that camp, I was there with a girl that I was-- Hanka-- that I was very close.
She had red cheeks.
And whenever they needed to pick somebody to work, they thought that she was stronger.
And she always was picked to a job.
I remember she always saved a little of her bread to share with me when she came back to the camp.
And it was a great help.
And we talked to each other that, by the first opportunity, we are going to try to run away.
And we passed the forest, and Hanka ran a way.
And there I was left without her.
And then when we passed another forest, we-- I ran away.
But little did I know, it wasn't a forest.
It was just a triangle of trees.
And there I ran in into some Germans.
And they took me by my collar, and they schlepped me back to the transport.
And I thought that, by God, I am going to be shot just this time.
But evidently, I was meant to live to be able to tell my story.
And the German just gave me a few hard kicks.
And I joined in to the working transport.
When the evening came close, they put all the prisoners into a barn, a few barns, and locked the doors.
I don't know what power possessed me and three other girls.
We lifted out the barn door from the hinges.
The Germans were evidently at the farmer's house having a good time there.
And I and three other girls ran away.
The other people didn't even want to run.
They didn't know where to go.
They were afraid, because the Americans were shooting from one side.
And the Germans were shooting from the other side.
And we went into a forest, and we found ourselves in such a situation, with the two armies shooting through.
And with my scouting experience, I knew that this was the worst situation we can be in.
The forest could catch on fire, and we could just get killed, right when we smell the freedom.
So I told the girls, we have to kind of crawl out on our stomach, and everybody should go the different directions.
If we get caught, we shouldn't all get caught together.
Maybe some of us will survive.
And I went into a farmer's house.
And her men were on the phone-- the son and the husband.
And she let me in, and she let me sleep over the night with the pigs.
And I remember that I shared the potatoes with the pigs, and nothing ever tasted so good.
She was so excited with the [NON-ENGLISH] coming, which is the enemy coming, that she didn't even give me anything else to eat.
She just told me-- when I told her that I am a Polish worker-- I didn't tell her that I was Jewish-- that I am a Polish worker that was in a work camp.
So she told me that she has a Pole that is in a-- a prison-- American prison military camp-- POW.
And he is-- it is in that town.
It was called the Vohl, that village that she was located.
And the man works for her during the day.
And sure enough, in the morning, that Polish fellow came, and we were talking with each other.
I didn't tell him that I am Jewish either.
In the last camp, when we were sent from Bergen-Belsen, they gave us a regular coat with-- the back was cut out, and there were crosses.
But I sewed that back up, and I sewed the cross on.
So when I ran away, I took the cross off so I had like almost civilian clothes.
And we were talking with each other, with that young man.
And the American tanks walked in.
They drove in, and I never saw anything so [SOB] beautiful.
And I remember that American soldier that was with me.
He jumped on the tank, and there was such a joy.
And we went into the town, that we were in a camp by Turkheim.
And then we went in to the town Turkheim.
And I remember, they gave me a place by a German-- farm-- where a German family, he was a father with two daughters.
And he-- they told him to take very good care of me, that nothing should happen to me.
And they sure did take good care of me.
And there, I slept over the first night-- liberated.
This was the end of April-- April 29.
It's strange that I don't remember now.
But I remember that when I went in, and I-- --slept the first night in a bed.
And I heard the German's daughter call him-- [CRYING] [NON-ENGLISH]-- which means father.
I always hope you realized what happened to me.
I was so absorbed with the desire and craving to live over Hitler's war.
But when I lived it over, I really didn't want to live.
[CRYING] And I remember those-- and I cried hysterically.
And I remembered those girls watching me.
They were afraid that I'm going to do something-- not that they love me so much.
But they did not want to hold the responsibility.
[CRYING] And I lived over the war, and I realized my losses.
And I was very disappointed with the world, that they let all those things happen to us.
And I remember we were passing Germany, being transported.
And the next day, the Germans were saying that they didn't know nothing.
And before that they didn't want to give us a drink of water.
So the world was just too cruel for me.
But later on, as they came by, some of the people that ran away came back to that little town.
And even that girlfriend, that Hanka, came back looking for me.
And we found each other.
And we again got organized.
And I joined the movement of the Bricha, which was the Israeli soldiers that were connected with the English Army.
This was the Palestinian Jewish soldiers-- not Israeli.
And-- because at that time, Israel didn't exist yet.
And I remember we were gathering.
At that time, I was already over 20.
And we were gathering the young people under 18 to send them with the [NON-ENGLISH] to Palestine.
And then, May and June came.
And I was taking the transport of the [NON-ENGLISH] to Minhen.
And there, I met a girl from my hometown that I met in Bergen-Belsen.
And when I showed her my group picture, her sister was there.
So she wanted me to cut out the little head from her sister.
And I did that for her, because she didn't have any pictures of the relatives, while I could warm my heart with looking at pictures of my father and of my mother.
They were tiny, but they still gave me something that I felt that I came from-- not just from stones.
[CRYING] And later on-- [CRYING] --the losses were great.
And I was inconsolable.
But still, life was going on.
And I was fortunate to hear in Minhen, that my brother is alive in Bergen-Belsen, because that girl that I gave her the picture of the sister was found by her brothers in Minhen.
And I met her.
And she told me that my brother is alive in Bergen-Belsen-- that he's very, very sick.
I should go there as soon as possible, and maybe I will still find him alive.
Without any delay, there were the American occupation.
And Bergen-Belsen was in the English occupation.
And there was a very fine American soldier that said, I'm a young girl, and I shouldn't travel alone.
And there were two young Jewish men that wanted to go looking for their family too.
And they assisted me.
And all the roads were still bombed, and there wasn't a good transportation.
And he gave me a letter that the American soldiers, at every point, should send me as far as they can.
And in three days, I managed to arrive to Bergen-Belsen.
And I remember a point, that my girlfriend that I met in Minhen, was wearing a watch.
When I saw her, she practically fainted and pointed to the watch.
She had a little two-inch piece of paper-- that my brother said, I am in block 10.
Come if you can.
She told him that she is going to find me.
And she showed him she had a watch that didn't ever work.
And she put it in there instead of the work, and she gave me that.
So I came there, and I knew exactly where-- where to look for my brother.
And I came there, and I send in the two boys, because I was afraid that he's going to get a shock if he is going to-- if I walk in on him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because the-- the-- the last time I-- I talked with him was in-- in Poland when I arrived to Piotrkow.
I made a telephone to Tomaszow.
And the next day, they rounded up all the people that were a day before on the telephone.
And they shot them all, and all spies.
But luckily, my brother wasn't between them.
It was the first day he went for a job that the Jewish police send him.
So we heard that they shot all the people that talk.
And my father and I sat Shiva in mourning after my brother.
And this was the last time I talk with him.
So-- but later on, I heard that he survived that situation.
So I didn't want to shock him, and they went in there.
And believe it or not, he disappeared a day before I arrived.
He kept running out looking for me, thinking that he's going to find me.
And he wrote letters on the wall.
And they kept finding him, because he was so weak.
He was after the typhus.
And he was very sick.
And then they couldn't find him anymore.
And we looked.
I looked for him in all the hospitals, and in the morgues, and every place.
Because a day before, he was there, and there he disappeared.
And then they told me that they are sending transports to Sweden.
Maybe he went to a transport to Sweden.
But he wasn't registered in Bergen-Belsen.
So I went to Lubeck, where the transports were going on.
Any sure enough, he was there on the list going to Sweden.
And I followed him right with the boat-- the next boat to Sweden.
Excuse me.
[CRYING] And when I arrived in Sweden-- and I arrived in Landskrona.
And the wonderful Swedish people did all the research.
And they found out that he was in Malmo, which was just not very far away.
Because Sweden is a very long country.
And just in a few days later, we called my brother.
He did not want to go to the telephone.
He has-- he said he has nobody alive.
He just did not want to get disappointed again.
But later, they convinced him, and I came.
And then we saw each other in person.
And then he became very sick on TB, and I almost lost him.
He was sick since 1945 through 1948.
He was very, very sick.
And thank God he survived.
And later on, Sweden was a country where the Swedish people rescued more women than men.
So there were more women than Jewish men.
And my husband-- my-- my brother got involved with a very nice Jewish girl.
But he decided that even though he was older, I am supposed to get married before.
[LAUGHS] And I remember in the meantime, I was keeping up on my time and working to support him.
And I was going to school, taking all kinds of courses.
And of course, I wanted to go to Israel something badly.
But he was so sick that I couldn't leave him.
But then, when there was the war of liberation, I decided that this is my time to live.
And I want to go to Israel to help out there.
There were volunteers and other thing.
And what did my brother do?
He packed his back, and he came to the town where I was living.
And he said, throw me out of the street.
I don't want a sister a hero.
I want a sister alive.
You are the only relative that I have left, and you are not going to go.
So I stood.
And I found my husband.
And we got married in 1949.
And my brother got engaged on our wedding.
And life was going on.
I did not want to have any children, because I felt that the world isn't good enough.
My brother-- OK.
